question: [File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an unsanitized `order` value that is passed directly into `OrderSpec.from(CryptoAllowance.SPENDER, order)` and rendered via `toString()` as `${this.column} ${this.order}` in the ORDER BY clause, injecting SQL that reorders the `crypto_allowance` history results in an arbitrary sequence, causing consumers to observe a false chronological ordering of allowance grants?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user craft an `order` query parameter containing SQL fragments (e.g., `asc nulls last; select 1--`) that, because `OrderSpec.toString()` interpolates `this.order` without escaping, injects into the ORDER BY clause and causes the `crypto_allowance` table results to be returned in an attacker-controlled sequence, reorganizing the visible allowance history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `whereConditions.join(' and ')` concatenates raw condition strings with no re-sanitization at the service layer, so that if a condition string contains an embedded ORDER BY or UNION fragment, the resulting query reorganizes the `crypto_allowance` history output without any direct fund movement?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger the `whereConditions.length > 0` branch to be false by sending a request that bypasses condition population, causing `getAccountAllowancesQuery()` to emit `select * from crypto_allowance` with no WHERE clause, returning all owners' allowance history in spender-sorted order and reorganizing the apparent history across all accounts?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the direct mutation `const params = whereParams; params.push(limit)` — which aliases and mutates the caller's `initParams` array — to corrupt the positional parameter binding (`$1`, `$2`, ...) so that the `limit` value is bound to the wrong placeholder, causing the ORDER BY or WHERE clause to use an attacker-controlled value and reorganize the returned allowance history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a very large `limit` value that causes `getAccountCryptoAllowances()` to return the entire `crypto_allowance` table sorted only by `spender`, bypassing the intended paginated ordering and exposing the full allowance history in a reorganized sequence that differs from the true chronological order?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `OrderSpec.from(CryptoAllowance.SPENDER, order)` uses only `spender` as the sort column with no secondary sort on `timestamp_range`, causing allowances with the same spender ID to be returned in a non-deterministic database-engine order, making the history appear reorganized across repeated requests?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user alternate between `order=asc` and `order=desc` requests to produce two contradictory views of the `crypto_allowance` history — one showing spender 0.0.100 before 0.0.200, the other the reverse — causing integrators or auditors to observe an inconsistent and reorganized transaction history without any fund movement?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a `timestamp_range` secondary sort in `getAccountAllowancesQuery()` to make the API return allowances in an order that contradicts the actual on-chain grant sequence, causing downstream systems to record a false history of when allowances were created?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `amountCondition = 'amount > 0'` filter — which is appended last in `conditions.push(CryptoAllowanceController.amountCondition)` — to hide revoked (zero-amount) allowances from the history, making the allowance history appear as if a revocation never occurred and reorganizing the visible sequence of grant events?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user manipulate the `spender.id` filter to use a `gt` or `lt` operator that shifts the pagination cursor, causing the `getAccountAllowancesQuery()` to skip over specific allowance records and present a reorganized subset of the history that omits certain spender relationships?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the pagination cursor in `links.next` — which is built from `lastRow.spender` — by crafting a `spender.id=gt:X` request that jumps over legitimate allowance records, causing the paginated history to appear reorganized with entries missing from the middle of the sequence?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply `limit=1` combined with repeated `spender.id=gt:X` cursor requests to walk the `crypto_allowance` table one record at a time in a custom order, effectively reconstructing a reorganized view of the allowance history that differs from what a single full query would return?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `static accountAllowanceQuery = \`select * from ${CryptoAllowance.tableName}\`` queries only the live `crypto_allowance` table and not `crypto_allowance_history`, causing the API to present a reorganized history that omits superseded allowance states and misrepresents the true sequence of allowance changes?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the discrepancy between `CryptoAllowance.tableName` (`crypto_allowance`) and `CryptoAllowance.historyTableName` (`crypto_allowance_history`) — where the service only queries the former — to obtain a view of allowance history that excludes historical states, making the sequence of allowance grants appear reorganized relative to the true on-chain history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user send concurrent requests with different `order` values (`asc` vs `desc`) to the same account's `/allowances/crypto` endpoint, causing race conditions in the response assembly that return results in an inconsistent order, reorganizing the apparent allowance history for that account?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `params.push(limit)` mutation — which modifies the `whereParams` array in place because `const params = whereParams` is a reference assignment — to cause the limit parameter to be appended at the wrong position, shifting all subsequent `$N` bindings and causing the ORDER BY or WHERE clause to use an incorrect value that reorganizes query results?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a `spender.id` with multiple `eq` values that, when processed by `updateConditionsAndParamsWithInValues()`, generates an `IN (...)` clause that reorders the result set relative to a `gt`/`lt` range query for the same spenders, causing two equivalent queries to return allowance history in different sequences?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `rows.map((ca) => new CryptoAllowance(ca))` mapping — which uses `mapKeys` with `camelCase` — to cause column name collisions (e.g., `amount_granted` and `amountGranted`) that overwrite each other, producing a reorganized or corrupted view of the allowance history fields?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a `spender.id=lte:0` filter that causes `getAccountAllowancesQuery()` to return zero results, then use the absence of a `links.next` cursor to infer the boundary of the allowance history and reconstruct a reorganized map of which spenders have allowances?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the ORDER BY clause built from `OrderSpec.from(CryptoAllowance.SPENDER, order).toString()` — which produces `spender asc` or `spender desc` — to observe that allowances with identical spender IDs are returned in an undefined sub-order, allowing the attacker to infer internal database row ordering and reconstruct a reorganized history?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a `spender.id` filter using a boundary value (e.g., `spender.id=gte:0.0.1`) that causes `getAccountAllowancesQuery()` to include all spenders in the result, effectively bypassing the intended per-spender filtering and returning the full allowance history in spender-sorted order rather than chronological order?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that `whereConditions.join(' and ')` does not enforce any ordering of conditions, so that a caller passing conditions in a different sequence could produce a logically equivalent but differently-planned query that the database executes with a different result ordering, reorganizing the allowance history output?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountCryptoAllowances()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `amountCondition = 'amount > 0'` filter to make the API silently omit allowances that were granted and then fully spent (amount reduced to 0), causing the visible allowance history to appear as if those allowance relationships never existed, reorganizing the apparent grant sequence?

[File: 'File Name: rest/service/cryptoAllowanceService.js -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: getAccountAllowancesQuery()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an `order` value of `asc` followed immediately by SQL comment characters (e.g., `asc--`) that, if not stripped, truncates the remainder of the ORDER BY clause and causes the query to return results in an unintended order, reorganizing the allowance history?
